elements.sent-2411-002: The Generalized Hamming weights and their relative version, which generalize the minimum distance of a linear code, are relevant to numerous applications, including coding on the wire-tap channel of type II, $t$-resilient functions, bounding the cardinality of the output in list decoding algorithms, ramp secret sharing schemes, and quantum error correction.
elements.sent-2411-003: The generalized Hamming weights have been determined for some families of codes, including Cartesian codes and Hermitian one-point codes.
elements.sent-2411-004: In this paper, we determine the generalized Hamming weights of decreasing norm-trace codes, which are linear codes defined by evaluating monomials that are closed under divisibility on the rational points of the extended norm-trace curve given by $x^{u = y^{q^{s - 1 + y^{q^{s - 2 + \cdots + y$ over the finite field of cardinality $q^s$, where $u$ is a positive divisor of $\frac{q^s - 1 {q - 1}$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-005: As a particular case, we obtain the weight hierarchy of one-point norm-trace codes and recover the result of Barbero and Munuera (2001) giving the weight hierarchy of one-point Hermitian codes.
elements.sent-2411-006: We also study the relative generalized Hamming weights for these codes and use them to construct impure quantum codes with excellent parameters.
elements.sent-2411-007: Generalized Hamming weights for linear codes were introduced by Wei in 1991 \cite{weiGHW as a tool in characterizing code performance on the wire-tap channel of type II.}

elements.section-2411-005: Relative generalized Hamming weights of decreasing norm-trace codes and quantum codes
elements.sent-2411-245: Relative generalized Hamming weights were introduced to characterize the security of linear ramp secret-sharing schemes.
elements.sent-2411-246: The $r$-the relative generalized Hamming weight of a pair of codes $C_1$ and $C_2$ with $C_2\subset C_1$ is the smallest number such that any set of shares of that size allows to recover $r$ $q$-bits of information about the secret for any set of that size, and the $m$-th relative generalized Hamming weight of the pair of codes $C_1^\perp \subset C_2^\perp$ gives the smallest size of a set of shares that can determine $m$ $q$-bits of information about the secret \cite{matsumotoRGHW,geilrghws .}
elements.sent-2411-247: These parameters have been studied for many different families of codes \cite{BEELEN2018130, galindonested, geilRGHWReedMuller , and in particular, for one-point algebraic geometry codes \cite{geilrghws,christensennested .}}
elements.sent-2411-248: In what follows, we study the relative generalized Hamming weights of decreasing norm-trace codes and their relation to impure quantum codes.
elements.sent-2411-249: We start by defining the relative generalized Hamming weights of a pair of codes using the definition from \cite[Lemma 1]{luoRGHW .}
elements.sent-2411-250: The $r$-th relative generalized Hamming weight of $C$ and $C'$, denoted $M_r(C,C')$, is \begin{equation}M_r(C,C')=\min \{ \abs{\supp(D)} : D  is a subcode of $C$ with  \dim D=r, \; D\cap C'=\{0\}\}.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-072: \begin{equation}M_r(C,C')=\min \{ \abs{\supp(D)} : D  is a subcode of $C$ with  \dim D=r, \; D\cap C'=\{0\}\}.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-251: It is useful to note that the condition $D\cap C'=\{0\}$ is equivalent to the condition $D\setminus\{0\}\subset C\setminus C'$.
